ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers cooked chicken feet; the tiger-skin deep-fry-then-braise preparation is a specific sub-preparation not individually coded in FoodEx2. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for braised chicken feet identified in the SNOMED CT food hierarchy as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One skewer of braised chicken feet weighing approximately 80 g, typically served as a street snack or dim sum side dish.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken, feet, cooked (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked chicken feet used as the primary nutrient anchor. Values adjusted upward for sodium to reflect soy sauce and oyster sauce braising liquid typical of the tiger-skin preparation, consistent with Singapore HPB Foodo compositional guidance for braised poultry dishes. Input record values (energy 225 kcal, protein 18.75 g, fat 15 g, carbohydrate 3.75 g, sodium 562.5 mg) are consistent with USDA cooked chicken feet data plus braising-sauce sodium increment and are retained. Singapore HPB Foodo consulted for contextual validation of the braised preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for tiger-skin braised chicken feet. GI estimated at 25 (low) by analogy with other high-protein, low-carbohydrate braised meat dishes (GI range 0–30) from the University of Sydney GI Database. Carbohydrate content is 3.75 g/100g; glycemic load per 80 g serving = (25 × 3.0) / 100 = 0.75, which is negligible.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 562.5 mg/100g from soy sauce and oyster sauce braising
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a skewer (≈40 g) and request reduced-sauce or low-sodium soy sauce preparation, as a single 80 g skewer delivers approximately 450 mg sodium — over 30% of the 1500 mg daily target recommended by the Singapore HPB for hypertensive adults.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (3.75 g/100g) but high total fat (15 g/100g) may impair insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: The very low carbohydrate content (3.0 g per 80 g serving) produces a negligible glycaemic load of 0.75, making this food acceptable for blood glucose management; however, the high total fat content (12 g per serving) should be monitored as chronic excess dietary fat can impair insulin sensitivity in individuals with Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (562.5 mg/100g), saturated fat (4.38 g/100g), cholesterol (100 mg/100g), and trans fat (0.13 g/100g) from deep-frying
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this food sparingly: the combination of high sodium (450 mg per 80 g serving), saturated fat (3.5 g), cholesterol (80 mg), and trans fat from deep-frying collectively elevates blood pressure and LDL cholesterol risk; a braised-only preparation without initial deep-frying would substantially reduce the cardiovascular risk profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation; sodium exceeds 400 mg/100g RA threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this preparation as the deep-frying step generates AGEs that worsen joint inflammation; request a braised-only version and reduce soy sauce and oyster sauce quantities to bring sodium below 400 mg/100g.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Braised Chicken Feet (Tiger Skin), known in Chinese as 虎皮鸡爪 (Hǔpí Jīzhuǎ), is a popular Chinese snack widely sold in Singapore as a street food skewer and dim sum item. The preparation involves first deep-frying the chicken feet until the skin blisters and takes on a wrinkled 'tiger skin' appearance, then slow-braising them in a savoury sauce of soy sauce, oyster sauce, sugar, garlic, ginger, and chilli. This two-stage cooking method produces a deeply flavoured, gelatinous texture rich in collagen from the skin and connective tissue. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 225 kcal, 18.75 g protein, 15 g fat (4.38 g saturated), 3.75 g carbohydrate, 2.5 g sugar, 0.63 g dietary fibre, and 562.5 mg sodium. The high sodium content from braising sauces is the primary clinical concern for hypertensive and cardiovascular patients. The low carbohydrate content results in a negligible glycaemic load (GI ~25, GL 0.75 per 80 g skewer), making it suitable for blood glucose management in moderation. The deep-frying step generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), which is a concern for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis. Commonly served on a single skewer weighing approximately 80 g, this dish is a staple of Chinese hawker culture in Singapore and is associated with Cantonese dim sum traditions.
common_user_queries: How many calories in chicken feet?
Is tiger skin chicken feet high in sodium?
Is braised chicken feet good for joints?
Can hypertensive patients eat chicken feet?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values are consistent with USDA FoodData Central cooked chicken feet data and are plausible for the tiger-skin braised preparation. Sodium has been validated against HPB Foodo contextual guidance for braised poultry. No exact HPB Foodo entry for this specific dish was found; USDA SR Legacy adapted values are used. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adapted (not exact) source match.
notes: G3 SENTINEL VALUE DOCUMENTATION: The following per_100g fields are set to -1 because detailed data for this specific deep-fried-then-braised chicken feet preparation are not individually reported in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024: monounsaturated_fat_g (-1) — detailed fatty acid fractionation for this braised preparation not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or HPB Foodo; polyunsaturated_fat_g (-1) — same reason as monounsaturated_fat_g; potassium_mg (-1) — potassium not reported for this specific braised chicken feet preparation in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or HPB Foodo; vitamin_a_mcg_rae (-1) — vitamin A not reported for cooked chicken feet in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or HPB Foodo; vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (-1) — thiamine not reported for this preparation in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or HPB Foodo; vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (-1) — riboflavin not reported for this preparation in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or HPB Foodo; vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (-1) — niacin not reported for this preparation in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or HPB Foodo; vitamin_b6_mg (-1) — vitamin B6 not reported for this preparation in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or HPB Foodo; vitamin_b12_mcg (-1) — vitamin B12 not reported for this preparation in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or HPB Foodo; folate_mcg_dfe (-1) — folate not reported for this preparation in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or HPB Foodo; vitamin_d_mcg (-1) — vitamin D not reported for this preparation in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or HPB Foodo; vitamin_e_mg (-1) — vitamin E not reported for this preparation in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or HPB Foodo; phosphorus_mg (-1) — phosphorus not reported for this preparation in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or HPB Foodo; magnesium_mg (-1) — magnesium not reported for this preparation in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or HPB Foodo; zinc_mg (-1) — zinc not reported for this preparation in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or HPB Foodo; selenium_mcg (-1) — selenium not reported for this preparation in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or HPB Foodo; water_g (-1) — moisture content not reported for this specific braised preparation in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or HPB Foodo. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — chicken feet contain no meaningful vitamin C; confirmed negligible per USDA FoodData Central cooked chicken feet entry). trans_fat_g of 0.13 g/100g reflects minor trans fat contribution from the deep-frying step, consistent with USDA data for deep-fried poultry products. GI of 25 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for high-protein low-carbohydrate braised meat dishes; no direct GI measurement for tiger-skin chicken feet exists in the published literature. Halal status assumed based on typical Singapore hawker preparation; individual vendor certification should be verified.
